schools.summary: Paul, as in the first epistle, joins 'Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)' with himself — the three who had founded the church together; he writes again to the same beloved congregation without adding his apostolic title, on a footing of affectionate familiarity; the church is addressed as 'in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' — its life and being rooted in God and Christ together, the Father and the Son named as the sphere of its existence (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Paul, and Silvanus, and Timotheus, unto the church of the Thessalonians
in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
the greeting from the three
the church rooted in God and Christ

schools.summary: Paul's customary greeting: 'grace (free favour) unto you, and peace (its fruit)' — the twofold blessing that opens his letters, grace the source and peace the effect; 'from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' — Father and Son named together as the one source of grace, a witness to the divine dignity of Christ; grace first, then peace, for there is no true peace apart from grace (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

themes: Grace unto you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
the apostolic blessing of grace and peace
grace the source, peace the fruit
Father and Son the joint source

schools.summary: The strong expression: Paul is 'bound (opheilomen — under obligation, owe it as a debt) to thank God always for you... as it is meet (fitting, right, due)' — his thanksgiving not a mere impulse but a debt owed, fitting given their growth; 'your faith groweth exceedingly (hyperauxanei — grows beyond measure, abundantly)' — their faith flourishing under trial; 'the charity (love) of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth (overflows)' — mutual love growing along with faith, and in every member (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: We are bound to thank God always for you, brethren, as it is meet
your faith groweth exceedingly, and the charity of every one of you all toward each other aboundeth
bound thanksgiving for their growing faith and love
faith and love increasing together

schools.summary: Their growth was so marked that Paul 'ourselves glory (boast, take pride) in you in the churches of God' — holding the Thessalonians up before other churches as an example to be admired and imitated; the ground of his boast being 'your patience (hypomone — steadfast endurance) and faith' — their constancy and steadfast believing under trial, shown 'in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure,' amid present, ongoing sufferings, their graces shining the brighter against the dark background of affliction (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: we ourselves glory in you in the churches of God
for your patience and faith in all your persecutions and tribulations that ye endure
Paul glorying in their patience and faith
steadfastness under persecution

schools.summary: Their patient endurance is 'a manifest token (endeigma — evidence, proof) of the righteous judgment of God' — their suffering grace, and the persecution they bore, point forward to a coming just judgment in which God will set all things right; 'that ye may be counted worthy (kataxiothenai — accounted, reckoned worthy) of the kingdom of God' — their endurance preparing and fitting them for the kingdom, ripening them for glory (not earning it but being reckoned fit); 'for which ye also suffer' — their sufferings for the kingdom's sake, the path to the crown (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: which is a manifest token of the righteous judgment of God
that ye may be counted worthy of the kingdom of God, for which ye also suffer
endurance a token of God's righteous judgment
suffering the path to the kingdom

schools.summary: The ground of the coming judgment: 'it is a righteous thing with God (just in his sight) to recompense (antapodounai — repay, requite) tribulation to them that trouble you' — divine justice requires that the persecutors be repaid with the very affliction they inflicted; the retribution fitting the crime, affliction repaid with affliction, those who troubled the saints themselves troubled (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the assurance that God will repay the persecutors is meant to comfort the afflicted — they need not avenge themselves, for God's justice will surely requite their oppressors in due time; vengeance may be left to him.

themes: it is a righteous thing with God to recompense tribulation to them that trouble you
the justice of God in repaying the persecutors
affliction repaid with affliction
a comfort to the afflicted

schools.summary: The other side of the recompense: 'to you who are troubled (afflicted, persecuted) rest (anesis — relief, relaxation, ease) with us' — God will repay the afflicted saints, not with more affliction, but with rest, relief from their sufferings, shared with Paul and all the faithful; 'when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed (apokalypsis — unveiled, disclosed) from heaven with his mighty angels (the angels of his power)' — at Christ's glorious second coming, unveiled in majesty and attended by the angelic host as ministers of his glory and judgment (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: to you who are troubled rest with us
when the Lord Jesus shall be revealed from heaven with his mighty angels
the promise of rest to the troubled
at Christ's glorious revelation

schools.summary: The awful scene of Christ's coming: 'in flaming fire (the symbol of his glory and his consuming judgment) taking vengeance (didontos ekdikesin — rendering recompense, executing just retribution)' — Christ comes to judge, repaying the wicked; two classes named: 'them that know not God' — those who, having the means to know him, remained in willful ignorance, chiefly the heathen who rejected the light of nature and conscience; 'and that obey not (refuse to submit to) the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ' — those who heard the gospel and would not obey it, the gospel demanding obedience (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril: the gospel is not merely to be believed but obeyed; those who refuse submission to the Lord Jesus Christ fall, with the heathen who know not God, under the vengeance of his coming.

themes: in flaming fire taking vengeance on them that know not God
and that obey not the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ
vengeance on the ignorant and disobedient
the judgment of Christ's coming

schools.summary: The doom of the wicked: 'who shall be punished (pay the penalty, suffer justice) with everlasting destruction (olethron aionion — eternal ruin)' — a punishment everlasting in duration, not annihilation but eternal ruin, the loss of all good and the endurance of the penalty without end; 'from (away from, banished from) the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power' — the heart of the doom being exclusion from God's presence, to be banished from the face and glory of the Lord itself the deepest ruin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the essence of the punishment is to be cast away from the presence of the Lord; the loss of God himself, the source of all good and glory, is a more terrible thing than the fire — the deepest meaning of the everlasting destruction.

themes: who shall be punished with everlasting destruction
from the presence of the Lord, and from the glory of his power
the sentence of everlasting destruction
banishment from the Lord's presence

schools.summary: The bright side of that day: Christ comes 'to be glorified in his saints' — his glory shining forth in the redeemed, the saints reflecting and displaying his glory as the trophies of his grace; 'and to be admired (thaumasthenai — marvelled at, wondered at) in all them that believe' — Christ the object of wondering admiration as his work in the believers is fully revealed, the glory of his grace in them exciting the wonder of all; the Thessalonians included 'because our testimony among you was believed... in that day' (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: when he shall come to be glorified in his saints
to be admired in all them that believe... in that day
Christ glorified and admired in his saints
the saints the trophies of his grace

schools.summary: The prospect of that day moves Paul to pray: 'that our God would count you worthy (axiose — reckon you fit, account you worthy) of this calling' — that they may be found fit for the high calling to his kingdom and glory; 'and fulfil all the good pleasure of his goodness (plerose pasan eudokian agathosynes — fully accomplish every gracious purpose his goodness forms toward them)' — every good desire and resolve God works in them brought to full effect; 'and the work of faith with power' — the work faith produces, completed and made fruitful by divine power (Barnes, Robertson, Henry, Calvin).

themes: that our God would count you worthy of this calling
fulfil all the good pleasure of his goodness, and the work of faith with power
Paul's prayer that God fit them for glory
faith made fruitful by divine power

schools.summary: The ultimate aim of Paul's prayer: 'that the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you' — that Christ's name (his whole revealed person and character) may be honoured and displayed in the believers' lives, their fitness for glory existing for Christ's glory; 'and ye in him' — the mutual, reciprocal glory, the believers sharing in Christ's honour as he is honoured in them; 'according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ' — the whole work flowing from grace, not merit (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril (with Calvin): the mutual glory of Christ in the saints and the saints in Christ is wholly the gift of grace; from first to last it is grace, not human merit, that fits them for that day and unites them to his honour.

themes: that the name of our Lord Jesus Christ may be glorified in you, and ye in him
according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ
the mutual glorifying of Christ in them and them in Christ
all of grace
